metric_value: 6,712 tpmC at $65/tpmC (NT, 4-way Pentium Pro, Microsoft SQL Server)
notes: TPC-C record for NT servers as of November 1996; labeled price/performance leader by TPC

metric_value: 6,671 tpmC at $90/tpmC (NT, 4-way, Microsoft SQL Server)
notes: Close second to Digital in NT TPC-C; $25/tpmC higher price

metric_value: 8,311 tpmC at $95/tpmC (UnixWare, 4-way, Sybase SQL Server with Tuxedo)
notes: Higher tpmC than Digital's NT config but $30/tpmC more expensive; Aberdeen noted this was a benchmark-special config rarely deployed

metric_value: 5,774 tpmC at $198/tpmC (AIX, 8-way, Sybase SQL Server)
notes: 8-CPU Unix server outperformed in cost by Digital's 4-way NT at $65/tpmC

metric_value: 4,939 tpmC at $232/tpmC (HP/UX, 4-way, Oracle 7)
notes: HP Unix server at 3.6x Digital's cost per tpmC despite equal CPU count

metric_value: 1,496 tpmC at $320/tpmC (OS/400, 1-CPU, DB2/400)
notes: AS/400 single-processor at 4.9x Digital's cost per tpmC; represents proprietary midrange server market

metric_value: Exceeds 30,000 tpmC (Unix-based AlphaServer clusters)
notes: Aberdeen noted Digital led even the Unix high-end cluster market

metric_value: 2,400 tpmC at $242/tpmC (Compaq ProLiant Intel-based)
notes: Aberdeen Nov 1995 Viewpoint cited as baseline; Nov 1996 Digital result represents ~180% improvement in 11 months

metric_name: Server market sweet spot transaction range
metric_value: 2,000 to 15,000 tpmC (midrange); below 2,500 tpmC (workgroup); above 15,000 tpmC (high-end/enterprise)
notes: Aberdeen segmentation using TPC-C data; Digital targeting midrange sweet spot with 4-way NT

metric_name: NT server market volume distribution by CPU count
metric_value: Over 90% of all server systems sold are 4-way-or-less
notes: Key rationale for Microsoft's 4-way focus strategy; Aberdeen cites this as reason Microsoft deprioritized 8+ way SMP

metric_value: Three-pronged: (1) maximize 4-way performance via CPU tuning; (2) continue limited SMP beyond 4-way; (3) pursue high-end via performance clustering (Wolfpack)
notes: Aberdeen described this as a deliberate market economics decision, not engineering limitation

metric_value: Build highly-tuned 4-way NT servers and performance clusters to capture large midrange market segments; Microsoft-aligned
notes: Aberdeen gave Digital high marks for NT marketplace commitment and price/performance leadership

metric_name: Digital NT channel and services strategy
metric_value: Broadest NT product line; largest MS training org in world; 2,000+ Microsoft-certified specialists; tight Microsoft alliance
notes: Digital had more Microsoft-certified specialists than any other vendor by November 1996

metric_name: Aberdeen explanation for NT SMP scalability gap
metric_value: NT scalability limits beyond 4-way are not engineering-based but are a deliberate market economics decision by Microsoft
notes: Controversial claim at the time; Aberdeen argued Microsoft consciously chose to focus on volume sweet-spot

metric_value: Digital has clustering core expertise since VAX clusters (1980s); early NT cluster leader; high-availability clusters already at market debut
notes: Digital's VAX clustering heritage was a genuine technical advantage for NT cluster development

metric_value: Digital's $65/tpmC pricing driven by manufacturing economies of scale plus Internet-technology transaction handling that eliminates expensive Tuxedo front-end
notes: Specific attribution to Internet-technology approach vs. Tuxedo middleware was a key insight

metric_name: 4-way NT server TPC-C projection by end of 1998
metric_value: 4-way NT servers will at least double and perhaps triple in speed by year-end 1998, reaching 11,000-16,000 tpmC range
notes: Driven by next-gen CPUs: Alpha at 500 MHz, Intel Klamath at 266 MHz, Deschutes at 333 MHz

metric_name: 4-way NT server TPC-C by 1998 - actual outcome
metric_value: By 1998, 4-way Pentium II/Xeon NT servers exceeded 15,000 tpmC; prediction substantially correct; however DEC was acquired by Compaq January 1998 before full realization
notes: Intel Klamath (Pentium II 300 MHz) and Deschutes (Pentium II 333 MHz) shipped as predicted; DEC acquired by Compaq for $9.6B

metric_name: NT performance cluster deployment prediction by 1999
metric_value: More than 100,000 NT high-availability/performance clusters deployed by year-end 1999; NT performance clusters exceed 20,000 tpmC by early 1999
notes: Wolfpack Phase 1 (2-node HA) targeted late 1997; Phase 2 (performance) for 1998

metric_name: NT performance cluster deployment - actual outcome
metric_value: Microsoft Cluster Service (MSCS) shipped in Windows NT 4.0 Enterprise Edition 1997; Windows 2000 Advanced Server extended to 4-node clusters; HA clustering widely adopted by 1999 though performance clustering adoption was slower than 100,000 unit projection
notes: Wolfpack shipped on schedule; performance clustering (NLB) took longer to deploy than predicted; TPC-C records on NT clusters exceeded 20,000 tpmC by 2000

metric_value: Making a Digital NT server decision should prove irresistible to many IT decision makers; Aberdeen expects Digital to lead NT midrange charge
notes: Aberdeen's bullish prediction on Digital's NT market leadership prospects

metric_name: Digital NT market leadership - actual outcome
metric_value: Digital acquired by Compaq January 1998 for $9.6B; Digital's NT product line and expertise absorbed into Compaq; Digital did not achieve independent NT market leadership
notes: DEC was sold before it could realize its predicted NT dominance; Compaq then HP absorbed the assets

metric_value: 8-way and beyond NT systems: high-margin, low-volume; primarily for decision support, data warehousing, and consolidation servers
source_page: The Advent of 8-Way and Greater Systems
notes: Aberdeen characterized 8-way+ NT as niche market; prediction proved accurate — high-way SMP remained specialty segment

metric_value: NT 8-way+ systems will remain low-volume as long as NT is 32-bit; 64-bit NT needed for full high-end capability
source_page: The Advent of 8-Way and Greater Systems
notes: 64-bit Windows came with Windows XP Professional x64 (2005) and Windows Server 2003 x64 — roughly 9 years later

metric_value: NCR scaled NT on sixteen processors
notes: NCR's split from AT&T raised long-term viability questions per Aberdeen

metric_value: Sequent scaled NT to 28 processors; sold multiple 14-processor NT/SMP systems
notes: Sequent acquired by IBM in 1999
